name: Barney Josephson
abstract: Barney Josephson (February 1, 1902 – September 29, 1988) was the American founder of Café Society in Greenwich Village, New York's first integrated nightclub. Opening artists in 1938 included Billie Holiday, who first performed the song "Strange Fruit" there.

about_quotes.speaker: Nellie Lutcher
about_quotes.evidence: So I met Barney Josephson, and he was very, very, very interested in my coming to New York... So finally, when I did -- when Mr. Josephson talked to me and said, 'Nellie, you've got to come to New York,' I said, 'Well, I'll have to let you know.'

about_quotes.speaker: Mary Lou Williams
about_quotes.evidence: Barney Josephson, the owner of the club, did everything he could to try to get me to smile... Barney Josephson was very nice. I wrote the 'Zodiac Suite' during that period and he put on a concert at Town Hall.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: Barney Josephson talked to Barney Josephson...put me and Pete Johnson in there... Barney Josephson's band... when it would get slow, Barney Josephson walked up to me... he fired me.

about_quotes.speaker: Al Hall
about_quotes.evidence: And so after many, many years you found yourself working again for Barney Josephson... when we left to go to Chicago at the Pump Room and Barney didn't want us to leave then because

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: Barney? Oh Barney, yes, Barney Josephson. He only came to a couple of them, because, as he put it, “This is a lot of fun, but I live it every day. I can’t stand more.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Avakian George — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: Teddy Wilson played at a place called the Cookery, which was at 9th Street and University Place, which was owned by Barney Josephson, who used to own Café Society.
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019
